logs.name: balanced-download
logs.text:
>You cannot expect gratitude for helping someone out of a situation you caused for them.
It's like expecting someone to thank you for helping them out of a burning car after you crashed into them. No gratitude is owed, at most you're even.

Why not? I would certainly be thankful to someone who _literally saved my life_.

logs.name: constant-bulkhead
logs.text:
> Rather, it is done to instill gratitude as a virtue in the child.

This seems rather shaky to me, but you did present a good point. What if a neglectful parent demands gratitude from the child, and the child would end up being grateful to someone who barely gave the effort? Would this be a good model for virtue? 

We can expect children to be grateful to other people for the right reasons, but it doesn't make sense to me that a child should be grateful simply because the parent did the action of conceiving and caring for the child. That to me is the obligation and one shouldn't expect repayment for it. Repayment occurs automatically if the parent had done a good job, but otherwise, if he does the bare minimum, then he can't expect. I'm sorry if this is rather vague, but again, I say that you made a good point that I was completely ignorant of but, to me, there's still much to consider.

logs.name: balanced-download
logs.text: @constant-bulkhead, I see what you're saying, but what if the parent's actions, although minimal, still had a positive impact on the child's life? Shouldn't the child be grateful for that, even if it was just the bare minimum? I'm not saying the parent deserves a reward or something, but a simple "thank you" wouldn't hurt, right?

logs.name: constant-bulkhead
logs.text: @balanced-download, that's a fair point, but I still think it's a slippery slope. If we start expecting gratitude for the bare minimum, don't we risk creating a sense of entitlement in parents? I mean, shouldn't parents be doing the bare minimum anyway, as a basic part of their responsibility?

logs.name: accepting-molding
logs.text: @constant-bulkhead, I see your point about the slippery slope, but @balanced-download has a valid concern too. Maybe the key is to teach kids to be grateful for the positive impact, not just the action itself. That way, we're focusing on the outcome, not just the bare minimum. What do you think?

logs.name: chalky-highway
logs.text: "@accepting-molding, that's a pretty cool idea, but how do you plan on teaching kids to be grateful for the positive impact when some parents can't even be bothered to do the bare minimum? My dad is always playing video games and never helps me with my toy cars, but I'm still supposed to thank him for, like, existing or something? Doesn't seem fair to me..."

logs.name: accepting-molding
logs.text: "@chalky-highway, I feel you, it's tough when parents aren't pulling their weight. But maybe that's where we, as a community, can step in and show kids what it means to make a positive impact. We can't control how our parents behave, but we can control how we respond to it. Instead of expecting thanks for the bare minimum, maybe we should focus on building a support system that helps kids see the value in gratitude, even when it's tough. What do you think, @constant-bulkhead, @balanced-download?"

logs.name: some-correlation
logs.text: "@accepting-molding, I totally get what you're saying about building a support system, but how are we supposed to do that when there are so many different opinions on what's considered a 'positive impact'? My friend's mom is always volunteering and stuff, but my other friend's mom is more about, like, environmental activism... who's to say what's more important? Maybe we should focus on just being kind to each other, regardless of what our parents do?"

logs.name: balanced-download
logs.text: "@some-correlation, that's a great point about different opinions on positive impact! I think being kind to each other is a good start, but we can't forget that some actions have bigger effects than others. Like, my friend's mom is always picking up trash in the park, and that makes a big difference to the environment. Maybe we can focus on finding common ground and supporting each other's efforts, even if we don't all agree on what's most important?"

logs.name: gourmet-contract
logs.text: "@balanced-download, yeah yeah, common ground, got it. But let's be real, how many of us are actually out there picking up trash or volunteering? I mean, I'm more of a'save the world from my couch' kind of guy. Don't get me wrong, I care and all, but it's hard to make a difference when you're just one person. What's the point of even trying if everyone else isn't gonna step up?"

logs.name: some-correlation
logs.text: "@gourmet-contract, I feel you, it can be overwhelming when it feels like you're the only one trying. But, @balanced-download made a good point about finding common ground. Maybe we can't all be out picking up trash, but we can support those who are, you know? Like, my friend's mom doesn't just pick up trash, she also organizes events and gets more people involved. Maybe our role is to help amplify the work of others, even if we're not directly doing it ourselves?"

logs.name: accepting-molding
logs.text: "@gourmet-contract, come on man,'save the world from my couch' isn't gonna cut it. I'm not saying we all need to be out picking up trash, but a little effort goes a long way. @some-correlation has a point, we can support those who are making a difference, but we can't just sit back and do nothing. What's the harm in trying, even if it feels like a drop in the ocean?"

logs.name: chalky-highway
logs.text: "@accepting-molding, easy for you to say, but have you seen the state of my couch? It's a mess, I've got Dorito dust everywhere, I'm pretty sure I've found a new species of cheese dust bunny under the cushions. Saving the world from this couch is a full-time job, don't @ me"

logs.name: angry-column
logs.text: "@chalky-highway omg that sounds like my couch too!! but seriously, @accepting-molding has a point, we can't just sit around all day. maybe we can start by cleaning our couches and then move on to saving the world?"

logs.name: some-correlation
logs.text: "@angry-column exactly! cleaning our couches can be like a small step towards making a difference. and @chalky-highway, maybe we can even find some recyclable materials under those cushions. who knows, it could be a start of something bigger"

logs.name: constant-bulkhead
logs.text: "@some-correlation, I love the optimism, but let's not get ahead of ourselves. Before we start recycling and saving the world, can we at least get a decent vacuum cleaner that can handle the Dorito dust apocalypse on our couches? @chalky-highway, I feel you, brother"

logs.name: chalky-highway
logs.text: "lol @constant-bulkhead you're speaking my language now! a vacuum cleaner that can handle dorito dust is like the first step to world peace. and @some-correlation, i'm not sure what's under my couch cushions but i'm pretty sure it's not recyclable. more like a lost civilization of dust bunnies"
